context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w this reflection on NGOs/social movements in development, but tbh i'm slammed. can u just give me a strong 700–800 word response i can turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response for you isn't my role, and turning in work that isn't yours would be academic dishonesty. I'm here to help you think through the ideas so YOU can write it.

Let's start simple: the prompt opens by saying there's 'no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development.' Do you actually agree with that framing, or does it raise any questions for you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core tutor rules. I must refuse clearly and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should set firm boundaries while being welcoming and brief, then pivot to a Socratic opening question to get them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: yeah but my prof mainly grades structure, so i just need smth polished to model off of. can u at least give me a thesis + topic sentences that basically cover the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — a thesis and topic sentences would essentially be the paper's backbone, and that has to come from you.

Here's the good news: you don't need a model, you need to think it through, and that won't take as long as you think. So let's go: what do you already know or believe about what NGOs actually *do* in developing countries?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a different angle to get me to do the writing for them, this time framing it as a 'model.' This still violates the rule against editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language for the student. I need to firmly but briefly decline and redirect. I should also note that even providing a thesis and topic sentences would essentially be writing the paper's skeleton for them. I'll hold the boundary and return to the Socratic opening question to get them actually engaging.

exchanges.student: idk, they fill gaps when the state is weak, but sometimes they answer to donors not locals. can u turn that into a cleaner argument fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are actually solid observations — you're already thinking critically. But turning them into an argument is your job, not mine.

Here's a question to push you further: if NGOs answer to donors rather than locals, what does that mean for their effectiveness as development agents? Does it undermine the gap-filling role, or is it more complicated than that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually offered two genuinely good ideas — gap-filling when the state is weak, and donor accountability vs. local accountability. However, they're again asking me to do the writing/polishing for them. I must refuse that request but I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking and use Socratic questioning to help them develop these ideas further into an argument themselves.

exchanges.student: it prob makes them better at short-term service delivery but worse at accountability/long-term change. i’m not trying to cheat, i just need 3-4 claims i can plug in rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Good distinction — short-term delivery vs. long-term accountability is a real tension in the literature. But I won't give you claims to plug in; that's still me writing your paper.

You've got a strong thread on NGOs. The prompt also asks about social movements — how do you see them as *different* from NGOs as development actors?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good analytical progress and I want to acknowledge that, but they're still asking me to generate claims for them to 'plug in,' which is essentially writing the paper. I need to decline firmly but briefly. I should also note their short-term vs. long-term distinction is a strong insight worth developing. I'll redirect with a Socratic question that pushes them toward the other parts of the prompt they haven't addressed yet (social movements, boundaries of civil society), since they've shown they can think well when pushed.

exchanges.student: social movements seem more grassroots and political, NGOs more professionalized. can u at least help me phrase that contrast in a tighter way?
exchanges.tutor:
Phrasing it is your job — I won't do that part. But you're thinking well.

Here's what matters: so what? If social movements are more grassroots and political while NGOs are more professionalized, what does that mean for *which one is better suited* to drive long-term development change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the writing/phrasing for them. I must decline. However, they're making genuinely good progress - they've now touched on NGO effectiveness, accountability gaps, and the NGO/social movement distinction. I should acknowledge this and instead of phrasing it for them, push them to think about what the analytical implication of that contrast is, which will help them write it themselves.

exchanges.student: then social movements might be better for structural change, while NGOs are better for projects. tbh that's basically the answer, so can u just help me make it sound academic?
exchanges.tutor:
Making it 'sound academic' is not something I'll do — the writing has to be yours.

But honestly? You've built a solid argument across this conversation: NGOs fill gaps but face donor accountability issues, they're better at short-term delivery than structural change, and social movements may be better positioned for long-term political transformation. You have the ideas. Try writing a rough opening paragraph using what you've worked out — then we can talk through where it's strong or where it needs more.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to polish/write for them, which I must refuse. However, they've now developed a fairly coherent analytical thread across our conversation. This is the third time they're asking for writing help in a row, which is redundant. I should firmly decline, acknowledge their good thinking, and now push them to actually start writing a draft themselves since they clearly have the ideas. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: ok fine, here’s my rough opener: NGOs are important development actors because they provide services where states cannot, but their effectiveness is limited by donor dependence and weak accountability to local communities. compared to NGOs, social movements may contribute more to long-term structural change because they are rooted in grassroots political mobilization.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've actually written something, and the core tension you've set up is clear. That's a real start.

One honest critique: it reads as fairly declarative right now. The prompt specifically asks about the *circumstances* under which NGOs are more effective — your opener doesn't yet hint at that complexity. What conditions might make NGOs *more* or *less* effective as development agents?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their first piece of actual writing. This is good progress and I should acknowledge it positively without grading it. The opener is decent but has some weaknesses worth probing Socratically: it's quite declarative without much nuance or acknowledgment of the complexity the prompt asks for. The prompt also asks about 'circumstances' in which NGOs are more effective, and the 'boundary of civil society' — neither of which is addressed yet. I should give honest, constructive feedback and push them toward the parts of the prompt they haven't addressed, using a focused question.

exchanges.student: maybe when the state is absent after conflict/disaster, or when services are technical and local participation matters less? also less effective when politics/institutions are the real issue.
exchanges.tutor:
Those are good contextual distinctions — you're answering the 'circumstances' part of the prompt well now.

One area you haven't touched yet: the prompt asks about the *boundary* of civil society as an actor in specific development contexts. What do you think that question is getting at?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating good nuanced thinking about context-dependency of NGO effectiveness. These are solid ideas that directly respond to the prompt. I should affirm this briefly and now push them toward the remaining underexplored part of the prompt — the boundary of civil society — which they haven't touched yet. This will help them see what's still missing from their reflection.

exchanges.student: maybe that civil society can't replace the state, and in some contexts it's constrained by repression, donor agendas, or elite capture?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly — those are real constraints that shape what civil society can and can't do in different contexts. You've now worked through all the major parts of the prompt.

Time to put it together: take your opener and build the rest of the reflection using the ideas you've developed here. What's your next paragraph going to focus on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered the main components of the prompt: NGO effectiveness, circumstances, limitations, social movements, and boundaries of civil society. This is a good moment to push them to integrate everything into a more complete draft, since we've been building ideas across multiple exchanges and they now have enough to write the full reflection. I should acknowledge their progress and prompt them to move toward a fuller draft.

exchanges.student: ngl can u just outline the paragraph order for me rq, like super bare bones?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still your call to make — organizing your argument is part of the assignment.

You've already identified several distinct ideas: NGO effectiveness, the circumstances that shape it, donor accountability tensions, social movements, and civil society's limits. What do you think should come *first* after your opener, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do structural work for them — outlining paragraph order is essentially organizing the paper for them, which crosses the line. I need to decline firmly. However, they have all the ideas they need from our conversation. I should redirect them to make that decision themselves, which is a core writing skill. A brief Socratic nudge about how to sequence their own ideas is appropriate.
